sanskrit_devanagari:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः ।
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः ॥ ३६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
asaṁyatātmanā yogo duṣprāpa iti me matiḥ |
vaśyātmanā tu yatatā śakyo 'vāptum upāyataḥ || 36 ||

anvaya.meaning: by one whose self is unrestrained
by one whose self is mastered

translations.verbatim_quote: For one whose mind is unbridled, self-realization is difficult work. But he whose mind is controlled and who strives by appropriate means is assured of success. That is My opinion.

Yog is difficult to attain for one whose mind is unbridled. However, those who have learnt to control the mind, and who strive earnestly by proper means, can attain perfection in Yog. This is My opinion.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 857 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Supreme Personality of Godhead declares that one who does not accept the proper treatment to detach the mind from material engagement can hardly achieve success in self-realization. Trying to practice yoga while engaging the mind in material enjoyment is like trying to ignite a fire while pouring water on it.

commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-36-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)

positions.rendering: by the means stated — abhyāsa and vairāgya

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings refer upāyataḥ back to the abhyāsa-vairāgya dyad of 6.35 — yoga is attainable specifically by THIS method, not by arbitrary effort. The operational claim — that effort without correct method does not produce attainment — is preserved.

literal_meaning: Yoga is hard to attain by one whose self is unrestrained — that is my view. But by one whose self is mastered, striving with proper means, it is possible to attain.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.36 follows 6.35's two-handed answer with a structural condition. Yoga is duṣprāpa (hard to attain) for the asaṁyatātman — the one whose self has not been brought under restraint (the prior precondition of abhyāsa-vairāgya). For the vaśyātman (one whose self has been mastered), yatatā (striving) and upāyataḥ (with proper means / method), it is śakya (possible) to attain. Sankara's reading: yoga is hard to attain by one of unrestrained self, who has not been able to bring his nature under control by abhyāsa and vairāgya; but yoga is attainable by one whose self has been controlled, who strives by the proper means. Ramanuja's reading: the one of unrestrained mind cannot attain yoga; but the one whose mind has been brought under control, exerting himself through the means already mentioned, can attain it. Both traditions preserve: yoga is method-dependent. Trying-without-method does not produce attainment.
